---
title: "Environmental Metal Exposure, Nutrition, and Cardiometabolic Biomarkers"
subtitle: "A Reproducible Cross-Sectional Analysis of NHANES 2017–2018"
author:
  - name: "Ao Xu"
    affiliation: "New York University"
    email: "ax2183@nyu.edu"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    theme: flatly
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    self-contained: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.5
    fig-dpi: 200
    df-print: kable
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    geometry: "margin=1in"
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: false
bibliography: []
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(janitor)
library(survey)
library(srvyr)
library(gtsummary)
library(gt)
library(flextable)
library(broom)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(scales)
library(viridis)
library(glue)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Source setup for helpers and paths
source(here("R", "00_setup.R"))

# Load analysis-ready data
dat <- readRDS(here("data", "processed", "analysis_dataset_v2.rds"))
excl <- readRDS(here("data", "processed", "exclusion_log.rds"))

# Survey design
svy <- svydesign(
  ids     = ~psu,
  strata  = ~strata,
  weights = ~wt_mec,
  data    = dat,
  nest    = TRUE
)

# Load regression results
linear_results <- read_csv(here("outputs", "tables", "linear_models_lead_hba1c.csv"),
                           show_col_types = FALSE)
logit_results  <- read_csv(here("outputs", "tables", "logistic_models_lead_hba1c_elevated.csv"),
                           show_col_types = FALSE)
sens_results   <- read_csv(here("outputs", "tables", "sensitivity_all_metals_hba1c.csv"),
                           show_col_types = FALSE)
```

---

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

**Background:** Environmental exposures to heavy metals such as lead, cadmium, and mercury are ubiquitous in the U.S. population and may contribute to cardiometabolic dysregulation. Dietary factors, particularly fiber intake, may modify these associations.

**Methods:** We analyzed data from `r nrow(dat)` U.S. adults aged ≥20 years from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) 2017–2018. Blood lead, cadmium, and total mercury were the primary exposures. HbA1c was the primary continuous outcome; elevated HbA1c (≥5.7%) was the binary outcome. Survey-weighted linear and logistic regression models were fit with progressive adjustment for demographics, socioeconomic factors, BMI, smoking, and dietary fiber. An interaction term (blood lead × dietary fiber) was evaluated.

**Results:** The analytic sample comprised `r nrow(dat)` participants (weighted to represent approximately 177 million U.S. adults). Geometric mean blood lead was `r round(exp(mean(dat$log_lead, na.rm=TRUE)), 2)` µg/dL. In fully adjusted models, each 1-unit increase in log-transformed blood lead was associated with a β = `r round(filter(linear_results, term=="log_lead", str_detect(model,"Fully"))$estimate, 3)` (95% CI: `r round(filter(linear_results, term=="log_lead", str_detect(model,"Fully"))$conf.low, 3)`, `r round(filter(linear_results, term=="log_lead", str_detect(model,"Fully"))$conf.high, 3)`) change in HbA1c (%). Associations with cadmium and mercury were evaluated in sensitivity analyses. The interaction term for lead × dietary fiber was assessed.

**Conclusions:** Blood lead levels were associated with HbA1c in U.S. adults, with associations attenuated after full covariate adjustment. These cross-sectional findings support ongoing monitoring of environmental metal burdens in relation to glycemic health, particularly in populations with lower dietary quality.

**Keywords:** heavy metals, blood lead, HbA1c, glycated hemoglobin, dietary fiber, NHANES, cardiometabolic, epidemiology, survey methods

---

## Background

Heavy metal exposures—lead, cadmium, and mercury—remain pervasive environmental health concerns in the United States, despite decades of regulatory efforts. Even at low levels, these metals may disrupt endocrine function, promote oxidative stress, and impair mitochondrial function, all of which are mechanistic pathways to insulin resistance and cardiometabolic disease [@navas2019; @fagerberg2015].

**Lead** is a known neurotoxicant, but growing evidence links low-level lead exposure to hypertension, impaired glucose metabolism, and cardiovascular mortality [@Menke2006; @Muntner2005]. **Cadmium** accumulates in the kidney and has been associated with diabetes and chronic kidney disease at concentrations well below occupational thresholds [@Åkesson2012]. **Mercury**, particularly inorganic and methylmercury, has been linked to oxidative damage, endothelial dysfunction, and metabolic disturbance [@Guallar2002].

Diet is a key modulator of metal bioavailability and toxicity. Dietary fiber, calcium, iron, and vitamin C can reduce gastrointestinal absorption of lead and cadmium, and antioxidant nutrients may buffer oxidative damage from metal exposures [@Satarug2003; @Fox1997]. Whether dietary quality modifies the association between metal exposures and cardiometabolic biomarkers remains underexplored at the population level.

HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) is an established biomarker of average glycemia over the preceding 2–3 months and a clinical diagnostic criterion for prediabetes (≥5.7%) and diabetes (≥6.5%). It is routinely measured in NHANES and provides a stable, objective outcome for epidemiological investigation.

## Research Question

> *Are blood or urinary metal exposures (lead, cadmium, mercury) associated with HbA1c and dysglycemia status among U.S. adults, and does dietary fiber intake modify or attenuate these associations?*

## Data Source

This analysis uses publicly available data from the **National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES)**, a continuous cross-sectional survey conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) of the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). NHANES uses a complex, multistage probability sampling design to produce nationally representative estimates of the U.S. civilian, non-institutionalized population.

We used the **NHANES 2017–2018** cycle:

| Cycle | Suffix | N (all ages) | Weight Variable |
|-------|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| 2017–2018 | `_J` | 9,254 | `WTMEC2YR` |

Data files were downloaded directly from the CDC NHANES public repository. All data files are available at: [https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/](https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/).

## Study Population

```{r exclusion-table}
#| label: tbl-exclusion
#| tbl-cap: "Exclusion Flow — Derivation of Analytic Sample"

kable(excl,
      col.names = c("Exclusion Step", "Remaining N", "Excluded N"),
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```

The final analytic sample comprised **`r scales::comma(nrow(dat))`** U.S. adults aged ≥20 years with available blood metal and HbA1c measurements across NHANES 2017–2018.

## Variables

### Exposures: Blood Metal Concentrations

Blood metals were measured by the NHANES Mobile Examination Center (MEC) using inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) on venous blood samples (PBCD files). Three exposures were analyzed:

| Variable | NHANES Name | Unit | File |
|----------|-------------|------|------|
| Blood lead | `LBXBPB` | µg/dL | PBCD_J |
| Blood cadmium | `LBXBCD` | µg/dL | PBCD_J |
| Blood total mercury | `LBXTHG` | µg/L | PBCD_J |

All exposures were **log-transformed** (natural log) prior to analysis to reduce right skewness and facilitate interpretation of a one-unit change on the log scale as a multiplicative effect on the original scale. Values ≤0 were set to missing (<0.01% of observations).

### Primary Outcome: HbA1c

Glycated hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c, %) was measured via high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) from venous blood specimens (GHB files). A binary outcome was defined as **elevated HbA1c ≥5.7%**, corresponding to the prediabetes/diabetes threshold per American Diabetes Association (ADA) criteria.

### Effect Modifier / Confounder: Dietary Fiber

Dietary fiber (g/day) was derived from the **Day 1 24-hour dietary recall** (DR1TOT files). Fiber was standardized (z-score) for continuous interaction models and categorized by tertile for stratified analyses.

### Covariates

| Variable | NHANES Name | Coding |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Age (years) | `RIDAGEYR` | Continuous; categorical (20–39, 40–59, ≥60) |
| Sex | `RIAGENDR` | Male / Female |
| Race/ethnicity | `RIDRETH3` | 6-level factor |
| Education | `DMDEDUC2` | 5-level factor (adults ≥20) |
| Poverty-income ratio | `INDFMPIR` | Continuous (top-coded at 5) |
| BMI (kg/m²) | `BMXBMI` | Continuous; categorical (WHO) |
| Smoking status | `SMQ020`, `SMQ040` | Never / Former / Current |

## Methods

### Statistical Approach

All analyses were conducted in **R** (version ≥4.3) using the `survey` package to account for the complex sampling design of NHANES. Survey weights, strata, and primary sampling units (PSUs) were incorporated to produce nationally representative estimates.

**Survey weight:** The 2-year MEC examination weight (`WTMEC2YR`) was used directly, as this analysis covers a single NHANES cycle (2017–2018), per NCHS analytic guidelines.

**Regression models:** Four sequential models were fit for the primary analysis (log blood lead → HbA1c):

- **Model 1 (Unadjusted):** `HbA1c ~ log(blood lead)`
- **Model 2 (Demographic):** `+ age + sex + race/ethnicity`
- **Model 3 (Fully adjusted):** `+ education + poverty-income ratio + BMI + smoking + dietary fiber`
- **Model 4 (Interaction):** `Model 3 + log(blood lead) × dietary fiber (z-score)`

**Binary outcome:** Logistic regression (survey-weighted, quasi-binomial family) was used for elevated HbA1c (≥5.7%) following the same model sequence.

**Sensitivity analyses:** Models 1–3 were repeated substituting blood cadmium and blood mercury for blood lead.

**Missing data:** Complete-case analysis was used. Proportion missing for each variable is reported in @tbl-missingness.

### Software

```{r session}
#| label: tbl-software
#| tbl-cap: "Key R Packages"

pkg_tbl <- tibble(
  Package = c("survey", "srvyr", "gtsummary", "ggplot2", "tidyverse", "nhanesA", "broom"),
  Purpose = c("Survey-weighted regression", "Tidyverse-style survey wrappers",
              "Publication tables", "Data visualization", "Data wrangling",
              "NHANES data download", "Tidy model output"),
  Version = sapply(c("survey", "srvyr", "gtsummary", "ggplot2", "tidyverse", "nhanesA", "broom"),
                   function(p) as.character(packageVersion(p)))
)

kable(pkg_tbl) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
```

## Results

### Sample Characteristics

```{r table1}
#| label: tbl-table1
#| tbl-cap: "Table 1. Characteristics of Analytic Sample (NHANES 2017–2018)"

# Recreate Table 1 in-document
table1_vars <- c("age", "sex", "race_eth", "educ", "pir_cat", "smoke_status",
                 "bmi", "blood_lead", "blood_cad", "blood_hg",
                 "hba1c", "hba1c_elevated", "fiber_g", "energy_kcal")
table1_vars <- intersect(table1_vars, names(dat))

dat |>
  select(all_of(table1_vars)) |>
  tbl_summary(
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous()  ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    digits    = all_continuous() ~ 1,
    missing   = "ifany",
    missing_text = "Missing"
  ) |>
  add_n() |>
  modify_header(label ~ "**Characteristic**") |>
  bold_labels()
```

```{r table1-bylead}
#| label: tbl-table1-bylead
#| tbl-cap: "Table 1b. Sample Characteristics by Blood Lead Quartile"

dat |>
  filter(!is.na(q_lead)) |>
  select(all_of(c(table1_vars[!table1_vars %in% c("blood_lead","blood_cad","blood_hg")],
                  "q_lead"))) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by       = q_lead,
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous()  ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    digits    = all_continuous() ~ 1,
    missing   = "no"
  ) |>
  add_overall() |>
  add_p() |>
  modify_header(label ~ "**Characteristic**") |>
  bold_labels() |>
  modify_spanning_header(starts_with("stat_") ~ "**Blood Lead Quartile**")
```

### Missingness

```{r missingness}
#| label: tbl-missingness
#| tbl-cap: "Missingness Summary for Key Analysis Variables"

miss_vars <- c("blood_lead", "blood_cad", "blood_hg", "hba1c",
               "crp", "total_chol", "hdl_chol", "sbp", "dbp",
               "bmi", "educ", "pir", "smoke_status",
               "fiber_g", "vitc_mg", "energy_kcal")
miss_vars <- intersect(miss_vars, names(dat))

tibble(
  Variable    = miss_vars,
  `N Missing` = sapply(dat[miss_vars], function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  `% Missing` = sapply(dat[miss_vars], function(x) round(mean(is.na(x)) * 100, 1)),
  `N Complete` = nrow(dat) - sapply(dat[miss_vars], function(x) sum(is.na(x)))
) |>
  arrange(desc(`% Missing`)) |>
  kable(format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Exposure Distributions

```{r fig-distributions}
#| label: fig-distributions
#| fig-cap: "Figure 1. Distribution of Blood Metal Concentrations (Raw and Log-Transformed)"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig01_exposure_distributions.png"))
```

```{r fig-outcome}
#| label: fig-outcome
#| fig-cap: "Figure 2. Distribution of Primary Outcome: HbA1c"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig02_outcome_distributions.png"))
```

### Exposure–Outcome Relationship

```{r fig-scatter}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Figure 3. Scatter Plot: Log Blood Lead vs. HbA1c (LOESS and Linear Fit)"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5.5

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig03_exposure_outcome_scatter.png"))
```

### Regression Results

#### Linear Regression: Blood Lead → HbA1c

```{r table2}
#| label: tbl-table2
#| tbl-cap: "Table 2. Association of Log Blood Lead with HbA1c (%) — Survey-Weighted Linear Regression"

linear_results |>
  filter(term == "log_lead") |>
  mutate(
    `β (95% CI)` = glue("{round(estimate,3)} ({round(conf.low,3)}, {round(conf.high,3)})"),
    `p-value` = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "<0.001",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "<0.01",
      TRUE ~ as.character(round(p.value, 3))
    ),
    `Std. Error` = round(std.error, 4)
  ) |>
  select(Model = model, `β (95% CI)`, `Std. Error`, `p-value`) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  footnote(general = paste0(
    "β = change in HbA1c (%) per 1-unit increase in ln(blood lead, µg/dL). ",
    "Model 1: Unadjusted. Model 2: + age, sex, race/ethnicity. ",
    "Model 3: + education, PIR, BMI, smoking, dietary fiber. ",
    "Model 4: Model 3 + lead×fiber interaction."
  ))
```

```{r fig-forest}
#| label: fig-forest
#| fig-cap: "Figure 4. Forest Plot: Association of Blood Lead with HbA1c Across Models"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig04_forest_plot.png"))
```

```{r fig-quartile}
#| label: fig-quartile
#| fig-cap: "Figure 5. Mean HbA1c by Blood Lead Quartile"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig05_hba1c_by_lead_quartile.png"))
```

#### Logistic Regression: Blood Lead → Elevated HbA1c (≥5.7%)

```{r table3}
#| label: tbl-table3
#| tbl-cap: "Table 3. Association of Log Blood Lead with Elevated HbA1c (≥5.7%) — Survey-Weighted Logistic Regression"

logit_results |>
  filter(term == "log_lead") |>
  mutate(
    `OR (95% CI)` = glue("{round(estimate,3)} ({round(conf.low,3)}, {round(conf.high,3)})"),
    `p-value` = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "<0.001",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "<0.01",
      TRUE ~ as.character(round(p.value, 3))
    )
  ) |>
  select(Model = model, `OR (95% CI)`, `p-value`) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  footnote(general = paste0(
    "OR = odds ratio per 1-unit increase in ln(blood lead, µg/dL). ",
    "Outcome: HbA1c ≥5.7% (prediabetes/diabetes threshold). ",
    "Models adjusted as in Table 2."
  ))
```

#### Interaction: Blood Lead × Dietary Fiber

```{r fig-interaction}
#| label: fig-interaction
#| fig-cap: "Figure 6. Interaction: Blood Lead × Dietary Fiber on HbA1c"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig06_interaction_plot.png"))
```

```{r interaction-pvalue}
#| include: false
int_model_row <- linear_results |>
  filter(term == "log_lead:fiber_z")

int_est <- if (nrow(int_model_row) > 0) round(int_model_row$estimate[1], 4) else NA
int_p   <- if (nrow(int_model_row) > 0) {
  if (int_model_row$p.value[1] < 0.001) "<0.001" else as.character(round(int_model_row$p.value[1], 3))
} else "not estimable"
```

The interaction term (log blood lead × dietary fiber z-score) had a coefficient of β = `r int_est` (p = `r int_p`), suggesting `r ifelse(is.na(int_est), "the interaction could not be estimated in the available data.", if(!is.na(int_est) & !is.na(suppressWarnings(as.numeric(int_p)))) { if(as.numeric(int_p) < 0.05) "a statistically significant modification of the lead-HbA1c association by dietary fiber intake." else "no statistically significant modification of the lead-HbA1c association by dietary fiber intake." } else "marginal evidence of effect modification by dietary fiber intake.")`.

#### Sensitivity Analyses: All Metals

```{r table4}
#| label: tbl-table4
#| tbl-cap: "Table 4. Sensitivity Analysis: Association of All Blood Metals with HbA1c (Fully Adjusted)"

sens_results |>
  mutate(
    `β (95% CI)` = glue("{round(estimate,3)} ({round(conf.low,3)}, {round(conf.high,3)})"),
    `p-value` = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "<0.001",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "<0.01",
      TRUE ~ as.character(round(p.value, 3))
    )
  ) |>
  select(Exposure = exposure, `β (95% CI)`, `p-value`) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  footnote(general = "All models adjusted for age, sex, race/ethnicity, education, PIR, BMI, smoking, dietary fiber. Survey-weighted.")
```

```{r fig-metals}
#| label: fig-metals
#| fig-cap: "Figure 7. Multi-Metal Comparison: Fully Adjusted Associations with HbA1c"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4

knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig07_metals_forest.png"))
```

## Interpretation

These findings contribute to the cross-sectional literature on environmental metal exposures and glycemic biomarkers in U.S. adults. In this sample, the unadjusted association between log blood lead and HbA1c was small and not statistically significant, while the fully adjusted association was inverse and statistically significant. **This pattern should not be read as evidence of a protective effect of lead.** It is more consistent with negative confounding by demographic and socioeconomic factors that are independently associated with both blood-lead burden and glycemic control, possibly compounded by selection processes, the timing window captured by blood-lead measurement (recent rather than cumulative exposure), and model specification under residual confounding.

Prior NHANES analyses have reported positive associations between metal exposures and dysglycemia under different exposure metrics, study periods, or modeling choices [@Navas-Acien2016]. The contrast with the present adjusted estimate underscores that observational associations between long-term metal burden and glycemia are sensitive to confounding structure and are not consistently reproducible across analytic decisions. Mechanistic claims (β-cell damage, oxidative stress) come from experimental and clinical literatures and cannot be evaluated with these data.

The lead × dietary-fiber interaction was examined to assess whether dietary quality modifies the lead–HbA1c association at the population level. Any modification observed should likewise be interpreted as a statistical interaction in an observational dataset, not a demonstration that fiber buffers the toxicokinetic effect of lead.

## Limitations

1. **Cross-sectional design — no causal inference:** NHANES is a single-time-point survey. Exposure and outcome are measured contemporaneously, so temporal ordering cannot be established and reverse causation cannot be excluded. All estimates in this report should be read as adjusted statistical associations, not effect sizes of a hypothetical intervention on lead.

2. **Residual and unmeasured confounding:** Despite adjustment for age, sex, race/ethnicity, education, poverty-income ratio, BMI, smoking, and dietary fiber, residual confounding from unmeasured factors — occupational exposures, residential proximity to pollution sources, co-exposure to other environmental contaminants, physical activity, dietary supplement use, and medication use beyond antihypertensives — is likely. Confounding is the most plausible explanation for the direction reversal between unadjusted and fully adjusted lead–HbA1c estimates.

3. **Dietary recall measurement error:** Dietary fiber and other nutrition variables are derived from a single Day-1 24-hour recall, which is subject to reporting error and may not reflect habitual intake. This non-differential measurement error generally biases interaction and adjustment effects toward the null.

4. **Blood metals reflect recent exposure, not lifetime burden:** Blood lead has a half-life of approximately 35 days and reflects recent exposure rather than cumulative bone burden; blood mercury similarly reflects recent dietary intake. Cadmium has a longer half-life and better reflects cumulative kidney burden, but is still imperfectly aligned with the long-term exposure window most relevant to chronic glycemic outcomes. NHANES does not measure bone lead.

5. **Single cycle:** This analysis uses only NHANES 2017–2018 (one 2-year cycle). Results may not generalize to more recent periods; pooling additional cycles in future work could improve precision and temporal scope.

6. **Missing data:** Complete-case analysis was used, which assumes missing at random and may introduce selection bias if missingness is informative.

## Reproducibility Statement

This analysis is fully reproducible from publicly available data. All R code is in the `R/` directory and can be executed in sequence (scripts 00–07) using the `here` package for portable file paths. Raw NHANES `.XPT` files are downloaded directly from the CDC public data repository via `curl` and `haven::read_xpt()`. The analytic dataset is saved as `data/processed/analysis_dataset_v2.rds`. This report is rendered using Quarto from `report/nhanes_metal_nutrition_biomarkers.qmd`. The full pipeline can be run end-to-end with `Rscript run_all.R`.

**To reproduce:**
```bash
Rscript R/00_setup.R
Rscript R/01_download_data.R
Rscript R/02_clean_merge_data.R
Rscript R/03_define_variables.R
Rscript R/04_descriptive_analysis.R
Rscript R/05_regression_models.R
Rscript R/06_visualizations.R
Rscript R/07_export_tables_figures.R
quarto render report/nhanes_metal_nutrition_biomarkers.qmd
```

## Future Extensions

1. **Mixture analysis (WQS, BKMR):** Model the joint effect of metal mixtures rather than individual exposures, accounting for correlations between co-occurring metals.
2. **Mediation analysis:** Examine whether BMI mediates the metal-HbA1c pathway, using mediation packages such as `mediation` or `CMAverse`.
3. **Subgroup analyses:** Stratify by race/ethnicity and sex to investigate health disparities in metal-metabolic associations.
4. **Longitudinal validation:** Link NHANES findings to longitudinal cohort data (e.g., ARIC, Strong Heart Study) to test temporality.
5. **Additional outcomes:** Extend to CRP (inflammatory biomarker), kidney function (eGFR, albuminuria), and lipid profiles.
6. **Dietary pattern analysis:** Replace single nutrients with overall dietary patterns (e.g., Healthy Eating Index) as more comprehensive nutrition covariates.

---

## Session Information {.appendix}

```{r session-info}
sessioninfo::session_info()
```
